figures.caption: Figure 1: Topological Neural Operators operate on cell complexes (i) whose physical signals are cochains at multiple ranks (ii). A TNO layer (iii) couples ranks through fixed DEC operators (dk, δk) and learns the rank-wise channel mixing in four blocks, signifying gradient, curl, harmonic, and self maps, producing a predicted PDE field on the complex (iv).
Table 2: Additional steady-state benchmarks: test relativeL1 (%). †Runs with official implementation; bold: best, underline: second. EmmiWing is given per-variable (ps, τx,y,z) and aggregate (Agg).
Figure 3: Qualitative results for Anisotropic Darcy with per-face random tensor orientations. Coupled signals require disentangling physical quantities at multiple topological ranks simultaneously.
Table 3: Rank-input and component studies: test relative error (%); lower is better, bold: best, underline: second; ∗param-matched MPNN (w=272, ∼5.35M≈TNO 5.0M).

Figure 4: Qualitative ablation across three models: Vanilla MPNN vs. TNO (no harmonics) vs. TNO with harmonics / copresheaves on Anisotropic Darcy (left) and Advection Diffusion (right), for which we depict model inputs and targets (col. 1), model predictions (col. 2), and model errors (col. 3).

Figure 8: Coboundary matrices for the oriented complex. The matrix d0 maps vertex cochains to edge cochains: each row computes one oriented difference, e.g. [−1, 1, 0, 0, 0] gives x2 − x1 on [1, 2]. The matrix d1 maps edge cochains to face cochains: each row computes one signed circulation. For the face [1, 2, 3], the boundary relation ∂[1, 2, 3] = [2, 3] − [1, 3] + [1, 2] gives (d1y)([1, 2, 3]) = y12 − y13 + y23; similarly, (d1y)([2, 3, 4]) = y23 − y24 + y34. Hence, these matrices encode oriented incidence, not just adjacency.

Figure 11: Upper and lower Hodge–Laplacian channels on edge cochains. Starting from an edgesupported signal u ∈ C1(K), the upper channel first applies d1 : C1(K)→ C2(K), which computes signed circulation around each face, and then applies δ2 : C2(K) → C1(K), which returns this face quantity to incident edges through the metric-weighted adjoint route. This gives ∆↑ 1u = δ2d1u, a face-mediated edge-to-edge coupling. The lower channel first applies δ1 : C1(K) → C0(K), which measures the metric-weighted accumulation of the edge field at vertices, and then applies d0 : C0(K) → C1(K), which sends the resulting vertex potential back to edges by oriented differences. This gives∆↓ 1u = d0δ1u, a vertex-mediated edge-to-edge coupling. Thus ∆↑ 1 and ∆↓ 1 are two distinct same-rank propagation mechanisms: one couples edges through shared faces, the other through shared vertices. This distinction is the degree-1 DEC analogue of separating curl-type and divergence-type effects, and is precisely what is collapsed when edge fields are treated as ordinary graph features.

Figure 12: Maxwell coupling as cross-rank cochain transport. The electric field E lives on edges and the magnetic flux B lives on faces. Faraday’s law uses d1 to map edge circulation to face updates, while the Ampère–Maxwell law uses δ2 to map neighboring face fluxes back to edge updates, with source J . Together, these two typed updates form the block Maxwell system.
Table 5: PDE templates as typed cochain systems. The DEC column shows the structural skeleton of the equation, while the TNO column shows the corresponding learned nonlinear cochain map.

Figure 14: HTNO as a learned two-grid V-cycle. Bottom: the input field on a point cloud is lifted to a fine cell complex K (k-NN or Delaunay), giving an input cochain u ∈ C•(K) that drives the V-cycle. The pre-smoother T pre

Figure 15: Validation relative L1 vs. training epochs (left column) and cumulative wall-clock hours (right column) for HTNO and RIGNO on the three RIGNO steady-state benchmarks. Both models were trained under their respective official optimizer/batch settings; final test numbers correspond to the entries in Tab. 1.
Table 6: HOGNN reproduction across all 2D benchmarks: test relative L1 (%); lower is better, bold: best per column. “Best of ours” is the smaller of TNO and HTNO from the cited table.

Figure 17: Qualitative comparison on the GAOT-suite benchmarks (Poisson-C-Sines, NACA0012, NACA2412, RAE2822). Each row shows one median-test-L1 sample for the dataset; columns: model input, ground-truth field, and per-architecture prediction û followed by absolute error |û − u| for TNO, RIGNO, and HTNO. Per-row error panels share a common color scale, allowing residual magnitudes to be directly comparable across architectures. Triangulation on the airfoil rows uses a vertex-local long-edge filter to drop spurious convex-hull bridges across the wing void.
